  5. Reusable Mapping Options: If the “Enable Reusable Mapping” option was selected during migration, ensure that the standalone artifacts are deployed first before deploying the main Integration Flow.

For other manual activities for specific senarios, please refer to the section “Manual Activities Depending on Integration Scenario” in the P2P Blog from series

Conclusion:

Content-Based Routing (CBR) scenarios can be successfully migrated to Cloud Integration using the Migration Tool, preserving receiver determination, routing conditions, mappings, and adapter configurations. While most artifacts are migrated automatically, post-migration verification and manual activities - such as security setup and reusable mapping deployment - are essential. Following the recommended steps ensures that your integration flows continue to run reliably and efficiently in the Cloud Integration environment.

What Will Be Covered in the Next Parts of Master Blog Series:

In the next parts of this blog series, we will explore the following topics in depth with a detailed and comprehensive walkthrough:

  • Extended Receiver Determination using the Pipeline Approach
  • Operation-Specific Receiver Determination and Multi-Operation Scenarios
  • Content-Based Routing (CBR) – Synchronous Use Cases

entry.title: Using Client Certificate Authentication in SAP CPI HTTP Receiver Adapter
entry.content:
Introduction

Hi friends, in this blog lets see how to configure the HTTP receiver adapter using client certificate auth type and step by step explain how a PKCS#12 (.p12) certificate is stored in the Keystore, which I have explored in my recent Integration flow development.

What is Client Certificate Authentication?

Client certificate authentication, also known as a mutual authentication method / mutual TLS (mTLS), ensures that:

  • The client (SAP CPI) authenticates itself to the server

  • The server also presents its certificate to CPI

  • Both sides trust each other using certificates issued by trusted CAs

Unlike basic or OAuth authentication, client certificate authentication relies on X.509 certificates and private keys, not usernames or tokens.
For more information about client certificate authentication, refer to the link:

https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/client-certificate-authentication-outbound?locale=en-US&state=PRODUCTION&version=CLOUD 

Files Provided by the Partner

Our 3rd party client (receiver system) has provided 

  • certificate.crt — signed client certificate (after providing the signing request)

  • private_key.key — private key (We need to generate this via OpenSSL if it is a Production tenant)

  • certificate-chain.pem — intermediate/root CA certificates

To establish the connection, all three must be updated in the tenant keystore, since SAP CPI does not accept .key files directly, these files must be converted directly into a PKCS#12 (.p12) format.

Creating the P12 File

The .p12 file contains:

  • Client certificate (eg, certificate.crt)

  • Private key (eg, private_key.key)

  • CA certificate chain (Intermediate + Root certificates) (eg, certificate-chain.pem) 

Using OpenSSL /Command prompt:

Open the command prompt from the same folder in which all these files are stored. 

Enter the command below, and it will ask you to set the password. After you enter the password, it generates the p12 file in the same folder.

openssl pkcs12 -export \
-in certificate.crt \
-inkey private_key.key \
-certfile certificate-chain.pem \
-out client.p12 \
-name "client-cert"


Note: An export password is required and will be used later during upload into CPI.

Uploading the Certificate to SAP CPI Keystore

  1. Log in to SAP Cloud Integration

  2. Go to Monitor → Keystore

  3. Choose Add → Key Pair

  4. Upload client.p12

  5. Enter the export password

  6. Save

The certificate is now securely stored and managed by CPI.

 

Configuring the HTTP Receiver Adapter

In your integration flow:

  1. Add an HTTP Receiver Adapter

  2. Set Authentication to: Client Certificate

  3. In the Credential Name field, select the alias of the uploaded .p12 from the Keystore.

 

Security Best Practices

  • Protect the .p12 password

  • Use separate certificates for PROD and NON-PROD

  • Monitor certificate expiry dates

Conclusion

Client certificate authentication is a secure and reliable option. Using a P12 certificate from the keystore with the HTTP Receiver Adapter keeps authentication simple and secure.

Thank you for reading this blog. I hope this helps you with your integration. When I started this integration development, I uploaded all the keystore documents separately, but it still ended up failing. Also, I couldn’t find any reference blog on how to handle this. So, I want to share this with others. If you feel this is a long procedure (as I do), please share your ideas and feedback in the comments.

entry.title: Legacy Excel to XML Conversion in Integration Suite: Handling BIFF5/Excel 5.0 during PI/PO migration
entry.content:
Migrating from PI/PO to Integration Suite comes with unexpected challenges - like handling legacy Excel files from the 90s. In this first post of our migration series, I'll show you how to convert those old BIFF5/Excel 5.0 files to XML when standard approaches fail. 

The Challenge

While excellent resources exist on Excel-to-XML conversion in CPI (see posts by @engswee, @yusufbayasal, and @RoohiJain), they assume modern Excel formats (BIFF8/XLSX). The Excel files we needed to convert were too old for these standard approaches, failing with the error:  "The supplied spreadsheet seems to be Excel 5.0/7.0 (BIFF5) format. POI only supports BIFF8 format (from Excel versions 97/2000/XP/2003)": 

javax.script.ScriptException: java.lang.Exception: 
org.apache.poi.hssf.OldExcelFormatException: 
The supplied spreadsheet seems to be Excel 5.0/7.0 (BIFF5) format. 
POI only supports BIFF8 format (from Excel versions 97/2000/XP/2003)

This error tells us something important: our Excel file is in the older BIFF5 format (used by Excel 5.0/7.0/95), which Apache POI's standard `HSSFWorkbook` and `XSSFWorkbook` classes cannot read. 

The solution

Apache POI provides a specialized utility class called OldExcelExtractor designed specifically for extracting text from these legacy Excel formats.

Preparation

Download poi jar file and imported into our CPI iFlow. To download it, go to the download page of Apache POI https://poi.apache.org/download.html >> Find the latest stable release >> select Binary Artifacts (https://repo1.maven.org/maven2/org/apache/poi/poi/5.5.1) >> select poi.x.x.x.jar. 

Upload it to the iFlow under References >> Add >> Archive, select the jar file and add. 

Implementation

We implmented the conversion in two steps: 

  • convert the Excel file into CSV format
  • convert CSV format into XML

Of couse one can combine both steps into the groovy script. But we did so to reduce custom scripting to minimum. 

Convert Excel to CSV

The script below has a hard-coded assumption about column count (every 10th row). Please adjust it to suite your needs. 

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;
import org.apache.poi.hssf.extractor.OldExcelExtractor;

def Message processData(Message message) {
    byte[] bodyByteArray = message.getBody(java.io.InputStream).bytes
    OldExcelExtractor extractor = new OldExcelExtractor(new ByteArrayInputStream(bodyByteArray))
    String extractedText = extractor.getText()
    extractor.close()
    
    // Split into rows (OldExcelExtractor uses \n as row separator)
    def rows = extractedText.split('\n')
    def cvsData = ""
    
	//Convert tab-separated rows to pipe-separated format
	//Note: the legacy excel file has exactly 10 columns per row
    for(int i=1; i<rows.length; i++) {
        if (i % 10 == 0) {
            cvsData += rows[i] + '\n'
        } else {
            cvsData += rows[i] + '|'
        }
    }
    
    message.setBody(cvsData) 
    return message;
}

Convert CSV into XML

Here we used the XSD schema exported from PI/PO, and converting into XML skipping the first line. 

That's it. Old Excel formats shouldn't slow down your PI/PO migration. With Apache POI's `OldExcelExtractor` and the approach outlined above, you can automate the conversion of even the oldest Excel files - no manual intervention required.

Coming up next in this series: More real-world migration challenges and the exact solutions we used to solve them. Have you encountered legacy format issues in your migration? Drop a comment below!

entry.title: Removing HTTP headers using PI web dispatcher
entry.content:
Hello everyone:

I have a client connecting to my PI server and sending the following HTTP headers, which apparently aren't accepted by my SOAP web service:

* Upgrade: h2c
* Connection: Upgrade, HTTP2-Settings

I'd like to know if anyone has encountered a similar situation and how they resolved it.

I've considered using the web dispatcher to suppress the headers that PI doesn't allow, which prevent the messages from even reaching the SOAP communication channel, but I don't know how to do it.

Thanks in advance for any help. Best regards.

entry.title: Mapping SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) Adapter to SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ Adapter
entry.content:
This blog post is part of the series Master Blog: SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite Migration – Tool and Guidance exploring how to migrate various integration patterns from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite using the Migration Tool.

Objective of this Blog

In this blog, we will focus on the mapping of SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter — with Transport Protocol “WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider” and Message Protocol both “JMS 1.x” or “JMS 2.0”) — to SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter.

Since the Migration Tool skips the SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter, the approach described in this blog will help to manually configure both Sender and Receiver IBM MQ adapters in SAP Integration Suite iFlows based on source JMS (IBM MQ) SAP Process Orchestration scenarios.

Related Blogs 

  • IBM MQ JMS Adapter for SAP Integration Suite
  • Integrating IBM MQ On-Premise with SAP Cloud Integration Using the IBM MQ Adapter

SAP Process Orchestration JMS Adapter Modules

In SAP Process Orchestration, the following JMS Adapter Modules are commonly used to convert message payloads between binary and XML formats:                       

  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertJMSMessageToBinary
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertBinaryToXMBMessage
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertMessageToBinary
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/SendBinarytoXIJMSService

In SAP Process Orchestration, these modules are often required when integrating with JMS providers such as IBM MQ. However, in SAP Integration Suite they are not required, as their functionality is provided directly by the IBM MQ adapter itself. The IBM MQ adapter in SAP Integration Suite offers built-in options for message conversion, allowing users to select either byte or text message formats directly in the adapter configuration.

Variants

Based on the SAP Process Orchestration source parameters, the migration will result in three different target variants:

- Sender

  • JMS Poll
  • JMS Subscribe

- Receiver:

  • JMS

Sender

The source Sender JMS MQ channel must have the following properties:

  • Adapter Type: JMS
  • Sender
  • Transport Protocol: WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider
  • Message protocol: JMS 1.x or JMS 2.0

The relevant Sender variants — JMS Poll and JMS Subscribe — are detailed below (REST variant doesn’t apply).

JMS Poll

JMS Poll implements a message retrieval mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Sender Adapter actively polls messages from IBM MQ queues or topics at defined intervals, using a request-based approach to check for new messages periodically.

Condition

In SAP Process Orchestration, for this variant to apply, the following condition must be met:

“Use Message Listener Based Connector” checkbox in the Processing view not checked:

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

The following table shows the mapping from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite:

 

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Process Orchestration

The following SAP Process Orchestration screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Source view

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “WebSphereMQ Cipher Spec”:

SAP Integration Suite

The following SAP Integration Suite screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Connection view

* After maintaining the Credentials in the SAP Integration Suite Security Materials section, the alias name must be provided in the Credential Name field to establish the connection.

Processing view

If “Message Destination” = “Topic”:

If “Message Destination” = “Queue”:

 JMS Subscribe

JMS Subscribe implements a message retrieval mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Sender Adapter establishes an active subscription to IBM MQ queues or topics, enabling real-time or near real-time message delivery as soon as messages are published to the destination.

Condition

In SAP Process Orchestration, for this variant to apply, the following condition must be met:

“Use Message Listener Based Connector” checkbox in the Processing view checked:

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

Same mapping as for the JMS Poll variant, but without fields 14, 15, and 16.

Receiver

The source Receiver JMS MQ channel must have the following properties: 

  • Adapter Type: JMS
  • Receiver
  • Transport Protocol: WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider
  • Message protocol: JMS 1.x or JMS 2.0

The relevant Receiver variant — JMS — is detailed below (HTTPS variant doesn’t apply):

JMS

JMS Receiver implements a message publishing mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Receiver Adapter enables SAP Integration Suite to send messages to IBM MQ queues or topics, acting as a message producer that publishes data to designated destinations in the IBM MQ server.

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

The following table shows the mapping from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite:

If “Message Destination” = “Queue”:

Advanced view

Conclusion

By following the mapping approach outlined in this blog — including detailed mapping tables and screenshots for the different target SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ sender and receiver adapter variants — users can successfully migrate their adapters from SAP Process Orchestration scenarios to SAP Integration Suite iFlows.

 * Since not all parameters can be mapped, end users must evaluate whether any functionality configured in the source SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter has not been migrated to the target SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter (e.g., PI Settings, Error-Handling Settings, or Duplicate Handling).

** In addition, some functionalities — such as correlation ID mapping — cannot be mapped directly but can be configured in the SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter. E.g., in the receiver adapter, it can be set dynamically using an exchange header or property.
